title: AI Engineering Core
description: Practical prompts about RAG, evaluation, and shipping AI features with clearer judgment.

cards.question: When do you choose RAG over fine-tuning?
cards.shortAnswer: I default to RAG when the problem is mostly about getting better context into the model instead of changing the model's behavior.
cards.expectedAnswer: I usually choose RAG when the main problem is access to current, proprietary, or domain-specific knowledge. It is faster to update, easier to inspect, and safer to iterate on because I can improve retrieval without retraining a model. Fine-tuning becomes more attractive when I need the model to adopt a repeated style, workflow, or behavior pattern that prompt engineering and retrieval are not reliably solving.
keyPoints: RAG is strong for current or proprietary knowledge
RAG is easier to update and inspect
fine-tuning is about changing behavior more than fetching knowledge
cards.learnMore:
A lot of weak answers treat this like a model choice instead of a systems choice. The better framing is: what problem are we actually trying to solve? If the issue is stale context, missing internal knowledge, or citation needs, retrieval usually gives you a cheaper and faster control point.

Fine-tuning starts to make sense when the model keeps failing in the same patterned way even with strong prompts and good context. Then the question becomes whether changing the model behavior is worth the operational cost.
commonTraps: saying fine-tuning is always better for domain knowledge
followUps: What failure modes make retrieval look better than it really is?
How would you evaluate a RAG system beyond latency?

cards.question: Why do evals matter more than a good demo in AI products?
cards.shortAnswer: Because demos prove possibility, while evals prove repeatability and help you catch regressions as the system changes.
cards.expectedAnswer: A demo shows that the system can work in a curated path. Evals show whether it works consistently across the cases that matter and whether changes make it better or worse over time. Without evals, teams end up shipping based on vibes, and it becomes very hard to understand regressions once prompts, retrieval, model versions, or guardrails start changing.
keyPoints: demos show possibility, evals show consistency
AI systems need measurement across prompts, models, and retrieval changes
cards.learnMore:
The real issue is not whether a demo looks impressive. The issue is whether the team has a way to notice drift, compare alternatives, and keep quality stable while the system evolves.

A production AI workflow usually changes in many places at once: prompts, tools, retrieval logic, model versions, ranking, guardrails. Evals are what stop that from turning into guesswork.
commonTraps: talking only about accuracy without task-specific criteria
assuming human taste is enough to manage regressions
followUps: What would you put in a first evaluation suite for a RAG feature?
How do online and offline evals complement each other?
